title: Queue overflow, backpressure, and channel capacity audit — opencode-desktop-dev

pubsub_unbounded.summary: All 8 PubSub instances use PubSub.unbounded(). Zero use of PubSub.bounded/sliding/dropping. No backpressure anywhere in the event bus or invalidation system.

findings.reasoning: Wildcard bus — ALL events flow through this pubsub. Any slow subscriber causes unbounded memory growth.
Per-event-type channels created lazily, one per event definition. No backpressure.
Invalidation event bus. Volume is lower than general bus but still unbounded.
Per-invalidation-scope channel. Created once per scope and reused. Void events only; bounded would be safer.
Core event bus 'all' channel — all events are published here. Slow subscribers risk OOM.
Per-event-type channels. Created on first subscription, never garbage-collected. If event types accumulate dynamically, this leaks.
Plugin 'added' notifications — low volume, only fires on plugin add. Acceptable risk.
Utility object merge function, not a queue. False positive from grep.

unbounded_concurrency_effect_forEach.summary: 19+ production sites use { concurrency: 'unbounded' } with Effect.forEach/Effect.all. These spawn unbounded fibers that exhaust memory/file descriptors under load.

findings.reasoning: Finalizer cleanup — runs once on shutdown, only as many runners as sessions. Acceptable because bounded by session count.

Inside a while loop that re-fetches after each batch. If a session has spawned many child jobs (e.g. tool calls), each batch cancellation spawns N fibers, and the loop can re-enter with another batch.
Background job cancellation — proportional to active background jobs. Discard=true mitigates backpressure but doesn't limit fiber count.
The session processor is a hot path. Unbounded concurrency here means N simultaneous LLM calls, tool executions, and state transitions.
LLM streaming path — called per-response. Unbounded fibers for event handling could accumulate.
Prompt resolution — each prompt part could trigger expensive operations. No bound on parallel resolution.
Resolves all prompt parts in parallel with no concurrency limit. A prompt with thousands of parts spawns thousands of fibers.
Tool registry internal operation. Depends on tool count but N is typically small (<100).
Ripgrep search results — large result sets spawn unbounded fibers for processing each match or file.
Another ripgrep path — same unbounded fiber risk on large result sets.
MCP server operations — bounded by server count but could spike on startup.
Same MCP path, bounded by server count.
MCP server cleanup — runs on teardown, bounded by server count.
Same path, bounded by servers.
Always exactly 3 effects (stdout, stderr, exitCode). Unbounded here is harmless because N=3.
Joins config dependency fibers. Bounded by config dependency count.
Skill loading/execution — bounded by number of skills but could spike.
Project-level operation — depends on number of projects/workspaces.
Another project path.
Control-plane workspace — bounded by workspace count.
Reference resolution — could handle many references in parallel. Unbounded fiber count.
ACP service — bounded by incoming request volume but unbounded internally.
ACP directory operations.
Worktree operations — bounded by worktree count.
Bootstrap runs once on startup.
HTTP request handling — unbounded concurrency in request handlers means memory exhaustion under load.
Same HTTP handler risk.
HTTP proxy — handles many concurrent requests. Unbounded concurrency is a direct DoS vector.

bounded_queues.summary: Zero bounded or sliding/dropping PubSub instances found across the entire packages directory. Every queue/channel in the event bus, invalidation bus, plugin system, and core event system is unbounded.

findings.reasoning: No bounded channels exist anywhere. All internal pub-sub communication is susceptible to backpressure-induced memory exhaustion.

custom_asyncqueue.summary: AsyncQueue in queue.ts is fully unbounded with an infinite internal array.

findings.reasoning: The internal T[] grows without bound if producers outpace consumers. Currently only used in `work()` helper which has a fixed concurrency limit (parameter). The AsyncQueue class itself appears unused beyond the work() helper which bounds its own concurrency.
Good pattern — concurrency is explicitly bounded by the caller. The worker pool pops from a fixed-size array, no unbounded growth.

stream_mergeAll_unbounded.summary: One Stream.mergeAll with unbounded concurrency found.

findings.reasoning: Only 3 streams merged (s1, s2, s3 from bus.subscribe). The unbounded concurrency here applies to the 3 stream sources, which is fixed. Safe in practice but misleading pattern.

flight_recorder_capacity.summary: Flight recorder uses a bounded ring buffer (capacity=1000) with intentional oldest-drop on overflow.

findings.reasoning: Good bounded behavior. Capacity defaults to 1000. Overflow drops oldest entry via events.slice(1). Configurable via FlightRecorderConfig.
Same pattern in wiredLayer — capacity=1000, drops oldest. O(1) write (slice creates new array but is O(n) — consider linked list for large rings).

cache_make_bounded.summary: Mixed: PtyTicket has a well-bounded cache (10k, 60s TTL). Account token cache and InstanceState use unbounded capacity.

findings.reasoning: Good pattern — capacity=10,000, TTL=60s. Eviction bounded by both maximum entry count and time.
TTL (1h) prevents permanent growth, but capacity is unbounded. Under heavy account switching, entries accumulate for up to 1 hour.
Keyed by workspace directory. If user switches between many projects, cache entries persist indefinitely. No TTL.

session_run_state.summary: Session run state uses a Map<SessionID, Runner> that grows with active sessions and has two unbounded-concurrency cleanup paths. No eviction of idle runners.

findings.reasoning: Runners are stored in a plain Map. onIdle callback deletes from map, but if onIdle is not called (e.g., abandoned sessions), runners leak until process exit.
Finalizer only runs once on instance shutdown. Bounded by session count at that point.

The while loop re-fetches jobs after each batch. Each iteration spawns unbounded fibers via `Effect.forEach(batch, ..., { concurrency: 'unbounded' })`. If jobs cascade (cancelling parent triggers child cancellations), the loop can iterate many times, each spawning N fibers.

recommendations.action: Replace PubSub.unbounded() with PubSub.bounded(capacity) or PubSub.sliding(capacity) in the core event bus (packages/core/src/event.ts lines 91, 99) and opencode bus (packages/opencode/src/bus/index.ts lines 68, 94).
recommendations.rationale: These are the central nervous system of the application. Any slow subscriber causes unbounded memory growth.

recommendations.action: Add explicit concurrency limits to Effect.forEach calls in hot paths: session/processor.ts:751, session/llm.ts:102, session/prompt.ts:236 and :1069, file/ripgrep.ts:245 and :402, and server route handlers.
recommendations.rationale: Unbounded fiber spawning is the fastest path to resource exhaustion under load.

recommendations.action: Replace the while+forEach loop in run-state.ts cancelBackgroundJobs with a single bounded-concurrency operation or a Queue-based approach.
recommendations.rationale: The loop can re-enter multiple times, each spawning unbounded fibers, creating multiplicative resource usage.

recommendations.action: Set a bounded capacity on account.ts Cache.make (line 246) and instance-state.ts ScopedCache.make (line 41) instead of Number.POSITIVE_INFINITY.
recommendations.rationale: Even with TTL, unbounded capacity allows memory to grow linearly with usage patterns.

recommendations.action: Consider replacing Array.slice(1) in flight-recorder.ts with a proper ring buffer implementation (modulo index) for O(1) writes instead of O(n) array copy.
recommendations.rationale: Current implementation is O(n) per write at capacity due to spread+slice allocation.
